1	# Video Script
2	
3	**Total Duration:** 8:05
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Hook — the poet nobody can find [0:00]
8	
9	Seven cities of the ancient world each swore that Homer was born on their soil — and not one of them could produce the man. He is said to have been blind, though the sheer visual dazzle of his verse makes even that doubtful. And lately a chorus of voices online has gone further still, insisting that Homer never lived at all, that the epics are a forgery stitched together by later hands. It is a strange accusation to level at the two poems Western literature was built on. Stranger still when you realize the poem answers it — because tucked into the eighth book of the Odyssey, singing at a feast, is a blind bard named Demodocus, whose song moves the hall to tears while the man himself sits unseen in the corner. Homer, it seems, painted his own portrait and hid it inside his masterpiece.
10	
11	## Thesis [0:55]
12	
13	The Odyssey is the first and greatest story ever told about coming home. And its author is the one figure in it who never arrives — a voice over the water, forever just out of reach. What Ithaca is to Odysseus, Homer is to us. We spend the whole poem straining for a shore we will never quite touch.
14	
15	## The life that isn't — and the mirror in Book 8 [1:25]
16	
17	Every great book seems to come with a life to explain it — a childhood wound, a lost love, a war. Homer gives us almost nothing. Around 700 BC, twenty-eight centuries ago, someone shaped these lines into their final grandeur — but that someone dissolves the moment you reach for him. He may not even have been a single person.
18	
19	And so, denied a biography, we look instead at what Homer chose to put on the page. He gave us Demodocus — a poet the Muse had touched, as the poem says, by taking his eyes and granting him "the sweet singing art." At the Phaeacian court, Demodocus sings of the fall of Troy, of the wooden horse and the cleverest of the Greeks — not knowing that the cleverest of the Greeks is sitting right in front of him, a nameless stranger. And Odysseus pulls his cloak over his head and weeps. It is the whole poem in miniature. The singer is blind. The song is more alive in the room than the man it's about. We could say that here, in a nameless bard, Homer tells us exactly who he is — a voice that outlasts its body, a maker who vanishes into what he makes.
20	
21	## Sung, not written [3:00]
22	
23	Because that is what these poems were — not books, but songs. The story and many of its very lines were handed to Homer by generations of oral poets who had sung them for centuries before anyone wrote them down. We know such singers were real: as late as the early twentieth century, scholars found bards in the mountains of the former Yugoslavia reciting poems as long as the Odyssey itself, from memory, without a single written note.
24	
25	Which reframes something readers have complained about for ages. Homer repeats himself — "swift-footed Achilles," "gray-eyed Athena," Dawn arriving again and again "with her rosy fingers," the sea forever "wine-dark." It reads like a poet running low on ideas. It is the opposite. Those fixed phrases are the machinery of memory, the handholds a singer grips to build a vast poem in real time before a live crowd. Even the shape we know is an afterthought: the Odyssey's twenty-four books, one for each letter of the Greek alphabet, were carved up some four hundred years after it was composed. The poem was a living breath long before it was a fixed text. And a breath has no single author. It belongs to everyone who ever drew it.
26	
27	## The wanderer earns his myth [4:30]
28	
29	Now watch what this vanished, sightless poet chose to make his hero. Not the strongest man at Troy — the cleverest. In a world that worshipped Achilles and brute glory, Homer built his second epic around a man who wins by wits.
30	
31	Trapped in the cave of the Cyclops Polyphemus, a son of the sea-god Poseidon, Odysseus does not fight — he thinks. He gives his name as "Nobody," blinds the giant with a sharpened stake, and rides to freedom clinging beneath a ram. Later, to hear the Sirens and live, he has his crew stop their ears with wax and lash him to the mast. Notice the pattern the blind poet keeps drawing: his hero triumphs not by looking but by *seeing* — by inner sight, by cunning so sharp that the help of Athena, goddess of wisdom, becomes impossible to tell apart from Odysseus's own racing mind. And notice his one great failure. Sailing away, drunk on victory, he shouts his real name back at the blinded Cyclops. That single boast lets Polyphemus curse him — and buys Odysseus ten more years lost at sea.
32	
33	## The ground under the myth — and the 200-year question [5:45]
34	
35	For a long time all of this was assumed to be pure fantasy. Then, in 1873, a merchant-turned-digger named Heinrich Schliemann struck gold in a Turkish hillside and declared he had found Troy — though the site had really been identified first by a British diplomat, Frank Calvert, whom history mostly forgot. Decades later, clay tablets in a lost script were cracked open: in 1952 Michael Ventris proved they recorded an early Greek. The Bronze-Age world the poem remembers was no blank — it had palaces, kings, and writing.
36	
37	So was Homer real? Back in 1795 the scholar Friedrich Wolf argued the epics had accreted from that older oral tradition, no single hand behind them — and turned Homer, overnight, from an object of worship into a two-hundred-year academic question that is still open. But here the honest answer matters more than the loud one. The poems sit between myth, literature, and memory. A tangled history of versions is not a forgery — no more than the rival manuscripts of a great classic make it a fake. The scholar admits the complexity; only the conspiracy-monger flattens it into "all invented." That Homer will not resolve into a single certain man is not a hole in the record. It is the poem, wearing the face of its poet.
38	
39	## Recognition and homecoming [7:00]
40	
41	Which is why the ending aches the way it does. After twenty years — ten at war, ten lost at sea — Odysseus comes home in disguise, and even home does not know him. His wife Penelope has held off more than a hundred suitors, weaving a burial shroud by day and unravelling it by night, buying three years with her loom. Odysseus is recognized at last not by his face but by hidden signs: an old nurse feels the boar-scar on his thigh; Penelope tests him with a secret only they share — a marriage bed he built with his own hands around a living olive tree, still rooted in the earth, a bed that cannot be moved. He is known, in the end, by what only love could know.
42	
43	## Close [7:40]
44	
45	The poem's own verdict comes from the underworld, where the ghost of mighty Achilles tells Odysseus he would rather be a landless servant among the living than king over all the dead. Stay alive. Get home. The journey, not the glory, is the thing. Odysseus is recognized, and let home at last — the one gift the poem quietly withholds from its maker. We will never dock at Homer. And perhaps that is exactly right. A poem about a voice carried home across the water should keep its poet out there still — blind, nameless, singing, forever just over the horizon.
46

# Video Script

**Total Duration:** 8:05

---

## Hook — the poet nobody can find [0:00]

Seven cities of the ancient world each swore that Homer was born on their soil — and not one of them could produce the man. He is said to have been blind, though the sheer visual dazzle of his verse makes even that doubtful. And lately a chorus of voices online has gone further still, insisting that Homer never lived at all, that the epics are a forgery stitched together by later hands. It is a strange accusation to level at the two poems Western literature was built on. Stranger still when you realize the poem answers it — because tucked into the eighth book of the Odyssey, singing at a feast, is a blind bard named Demodocus, whose song moves the hall to tears while the man himself sits unseen in the corner. Homer, it seems, painted his own portrait and hid it inside his masterpiece.

## Thesis [0:55]

The Odyssey is the first and greatest story ever told about coming home. And its author is the one figure in it who never arrives — a voice over the water, forever just out of reach. What Ithaca is to Odysseus, Homer is to us. We spend the whole poem straining for a shore we will never quite touch.

## The life that isn't — and the mirror in Book 8 [1:25]

Every great book seems to come with a life to explain it — a childhood wound, a lost love, a war. Homer gives us almost nothing. Around 700 BC, twenty-eight centuries ago, someone shaped these lines into their final grandeur — but that someone dissolves the moment you reach for him. He may not even have been a single person.

And so, denied a biography, we look instead at what Homer chose to put on the page. He gave us Demodocus — a poet the Muse had touched, as the poem says, by taking his eyes and granting him "the sweet singing art." At the Phaeacian court, Demodocus sings of the fall of Troy, of the wooden horse and the cleverest of the Greeks — not knowing that the cleverest of the Greeks is sitting right in front of him, a nameless stranger. And Odysseus pulls his cloak over his head and weeps. It is the whole poem in miniature. The singer is blind. The song is more alive in the room than the man it's about. We could say that here, in a nameless bard, Homer tells us exactly who he is — a voice that outlasts its body, a maker who vanishes into what he makes.

## Sung, not written [3:00]

Because that is what these poems were — not books, but songs. The story and many of its very lines were handed to Homer by generations of oral poets who had sung them for centuries before anyone wrote them down. We know such singers were real: as late as the early twentieth century, scholars found bards in the mountains of the former Yugoslavia reciting poems as long as the Odyssey itself, from memory, without a single written note.

Which reframes something readers have complained about for ages. Homer repeats himself — "swift-footed Achilles," "gray-eyed Athena," Dawn arriving again and again "with her rosy fingers," the sea forever "wine-dark." It reads like a poet running low on ideas. It is the opposite. Those fixed phrases are the machinery of memory, the handholds a singer grips to build a vast poem in real time before a live crowd. Even the shape we know is an afterthought: the Odyssey's twenty-four books, one for each letter of the Greek alphabet, were carved up some four hundred years after it was composed. The poem was a living breath long before it was a fixed text. And a breath has no single author. It belongs to everyone who ever drew it.

## The wanderer earns his myth [4:30]

Now watch what this vanished, sightless poet chose to make his hero. Not the strongest man at Troy — the cleverest. In a world that worshipped Achilles and brute glory, Homer built his second epic around a man who wins by wits.

Trapped in the cave of the Cyclops Polyphemus, a son of the sea-god Poseidon, Odysseus does not fight — he thinks. He gives his name as "Nobody," blinds the giant with a sharpened stake, and rides to freedom clinging beneath a ram. Later, to hear the Sirens and live, he has his crew stop their ears with wax and lash him to the mast. Notice the pattern the blind poet keeps drawing: his hero triumphs not by looking but by *seeing* — by inner sight, by cunning so sharp that the help of Athena, goddess of wisdom, becomes impossible to tell apart from Odysseus's own racing mind. And notice his one great failure. Sailing away, drunk on victory, he shouts his real name back at the blinded Cyclops. That single boast lets Polyphemus curse him — and buys Odysseus ten more years lost at sea.

## The ground under the myth — and the 200-year question [5:45]

For a long time all of this was assumed to be pure fantasy. Then, in 1873, a merchant-turned-digger named Heinrich Schliemann struck gold in a Turkish hillside and declared he had found Troy — though the site had really been identified first by a British diplomat, Frank Calvert, whom history mostly forgot. Decades later, clay tablets in a lost script were cracked open: in 1952 Michael Ventris proved they recorded an early Greek. The Bronze-Age world the poem remembers was no blank — it had palaces, kings, and writing.

So was Homer real? Back in 1795 the scholar Friedrich Wolf argued the epics had accreted from that older oral tradition, no single hand behind them — and turned Homer, overnight, from an object of worship into a two-hundred-year academic question that is still open. But here the honest answer matters more than the loud one. The poems sit between myth, literature, and memory. A tangled history of versions is not a forgery — no more than the rival manuscripts of a great classic make it a fake. The scholar admits the complexity; only the conspiracy-monger flattens it into "all invented." That Homer will not resolve into a single certain man is not a hole in the record. It is the poem, wearing the face of its poet.

## Recognition and homecoming [7:00]

Which is why the ending aches the way it does. After twenty years — ten at war, ten lost at sea — Odysseus comes home in disguise, and even home does not know him. His wife Penelope has held off more than a hundred suitors, weaving a burial shroud by day and unravelling it by night, buying three years with her loom. Odysseus is recognized at last not by his face but by hidden signs: an old nurse feels the boar-scar on his thigh; Penelope tests him with a secret only they share — a marriage bed he built with his own hands around a living olive tree, still rooted in the earth, a bed that cannot be moved. He is known, in the end, by what only love could know.

## Close [7:40]

The poem's own verdict comes from the underworld, where the ghost of mighty Achilles tells Odysseus he would rather be a landless servant among the living than king over all the dead. Stay alive. Get home. The journey, not the glory, is the thing. Odysseus is recognized, and let home at last — the one gift the poem quietly withholds from its maker. We will never dock at Homer. And perhaps that is exactly right. A poem about a voice carried home across the water should keep its poet out there still — blind, nameless, singing, forever just over the horizon.

1	# Style Guide: Homer
2	
3	A polished, accessible-but-rigorous video essay on Homer, the Odyssey, and the two-hundred-year question of whether "Homer" was ever a single man. An explainer-documentary hybrid for a general audience that wants the literature, the archaeology, and the scholarship — without academic distance. Runtime is short (8:05), so every section must earn its seconds; this is a tight, single-metaphor essay, not a sprawling survey.
4	
5	## Voice
6	
7	Conversational essayist with a literary ear. Addresses the viewer as a fellow reader straining toward the same unreachable shore ("What Ithaca is to Odysseus, Homer is to us," "We will never dock at Homer"). The register is warmer and more lyrical than a news explainer — it borrows the cadence of the poem it describes — but it stays rigorous: it names dates, diggers, and scholars, and it refuses the easy conspiracy answer in favor of the honest, complicated one.
8	
9	Educational posture, not advocacy. The live debate here is "did Homer exist / are the epics a forgery," and the script's job is to *analyze* that question, side with scholarly complexity over online flattening, and never sneer. Keep that even hand in every beat.
10	
11	Recurring rhetorical moves as this script actually uses them:
12	- **Open on paradox** — seven cities claim a poet none can produce; a blind man writes the most visual verse ever. Lead the hook with the contradiction, not the summary.
13	- **The controlling metaphor, not a numbered thesis.** This essay does *not* split into "three lenses." It runs one image the whole way: Homer is the Ithaca we never reach. Do not manufacture a numbered structure the script doesn't have — let the metaphor recur instead (Book 8 mirror → the sung breath with "no single author" → the poet "just over the horizon").
14	- **Quote the poem, not modern people.** The human texture here comes from Homer's own epithets — "swift-footed Achilles," "gray-eyed Athena," Dawn's "rosy fingers," the "wine-dark" sea, the "sweet singing art" — and from Achilles' ghost preferring "a landless servant among the living" to "king over all the dead." The named real people (Schliemann, Calvert, Ventris, Wolf) are cited for what they *did*, not quoted. Preserve that distinction.
15	- **Cadence phrase: "Notice…" / "Now watch…"** The script's recurring takeaway signal is the invitation to observe the blind poet's choices ("Now watch what this vanished, sightless poet chose to make his hero," "Notice the pattern the blind poet keeps drawing," "And notice his one great failure"). Use this as the key-insight cue instead of the template's "here's the key insight" — mark those beats visually.
16	- **End on reflection, not conclusion.** Close on the withheld homecoming — the poet left "blind, nameless, singing, forever just over the horizon." No thesis restatement, no call to action.
17	
18	## Look
19	
20	Cinematic, color-graded, premium — but with one hard constraint: **the project's source library is empty. There is no indexed archival footage to draw on.** Do not promise drone, news, or contemporary documentary b-roll that does not exist. The look must be built primarily from generated imagery and typography, with real imagery sourced deliberately rather than assumed.
21	
22	Combine these modes:
23	
24	- **AI-generated conceptual imagery (dominant mode)** — the workhorse here. Wine-dark seas, a blind bard singing in a torchlit Bronze-Age hall, the wooden horse, the Cyclops' cave, Odysseus lashed to the mast, Penelope at her loom, the olive-tree bed. Lean cinematic and photorealistic-with-a-mythic-cast; symbolic compositions land harder than any literal reconstruction. A recurring visual motif — a solitary figure/voice receding over water — should thread the hook, the Book 8 mirror, and the close.
25	- **Sourced real imagery** — where authenticity matters, pull from the picture library / asset extractors (Met, Wikimedia): Greek black- and red-figure vase paintings of Odysseus and the Sirens, the ruins at Hisarlik/Troy, Linear B tablets, early manuscript pages of the Odyssey, portraits/busts of Schliemann and the idealized blind "Homer." Treat these as museum-grade stills, not stock b-roll.
26	- **Text overlays** — for the epithets (set them as recurring typographic refrains), for the "Notice…/Now watch…" key-insight beats, and for section markers. Clean, typography-driven, serif-forward to match the literary subject.
27	- **Information graphics** — a map of the seven cities claiming Homer and of Odysseus's voyage across the Mediterranean; a timeline (oral tradition → ~700 BC composition → the 24 books carved up ~400 years later → Wolf 1795 → Schliemann/Calvert 1873 → Ventris 1952); the ram-escape and Nobody/name beats as small diagrammatic cards.
28	
29	Avoid the stock-footage look entirely — there is no real archival library to fall back on, so the burden is on generation quality and museum sourcing.
30	
31	## Pacing
32	
33	Total runtime 8:05 (~485s), eight sections. Average scene ~8–11s, varied by section. Tuned to *this* script's proportions, not the template's 10-minute default:
34	
35	- **Hook (0:00–0:55, ~55s)** — faster, 5–7s scenes, ~8–9 shots, to carry the seven-cities paradox and land the Demodocus reveal.
36	- **Thesis (0:55–1:25, ~30s)** — slow down to ~10–12s; this is the controlling metaphor and it must register. 2–3 scenes.
37	- **Body — Book 8 mirror / Sung not written / The wanderer (1:25–5:45)** — standard ~9–11s explanatory pacing. This is the longest stretch; enforce visual-type variety hardest here.
38	- **The ground under the myth (5:45–7:00, ~75s)** — steady, graphic-and-timeline-heavy; the archaeology and the Wolf question want maps and dates on screen.
39	- **Recognition & Close (7:00–8:05, ~65s)** — slightly slower, ~12s, for the scar/olive-bed recognition and the receding-poet ending to land.
40	
41	Visual-type variety enforced: no more than 2 consecutive scenes of the same `visual_type`. Each section opening gets a distinct marker — a text-overlay section card or a graphic beat.
42	
43	## Editorial
44	
45	- **Human stakes come from the poem's language**, not from quoting modern scholars. Set the epithets as recurring typographic refrains; give Achilles' underworld line ("rather be a landless servant among the living…") a dedicated text-overlay beat near the close.
46	- **The archaeology section leans on graphics** — timeline and map are near-mandatory for the 1795/1873/1952 dates and the Troy location. Name Frank Calvert on screen where the narration restores his credit.
47	- **Mark "Notice…/Now watch…" moments visually** with dedicated text-overlay scenes, not narration alone — these are the key-insight beats.
48	- **Echo the single controlling metaphor visually**, not a fake multi-part structure. Reprise the receding-figure-over-water motif at the thesis, the Book 8 mirror, and the close so the through-line reads.
49	- **No sustained talking-heads / no lecture stills** — always intercut generated imagery, vase art, or graphics.
50	- **Hold the even hand on the "forgery" debate.** Visuals should dramatize scholarly complexity (rival manuscripts, layered oral tradition), never caricature the online skeptics. Symbolic yes; mockery no.
51	
52	## Visual Type Vocabulary
53	
54	Tuned to this project. `generated-image` is promoted to the primary mode because there is no indexed footage; `b-roll` is retained but redefined for sourced museum/site imagery rather than a live library.
55	
56	- `generated-image` — AI-generated mythic/cinematic stills and short-animated imagery (the dominant mode)
57	- `b-roll` — **sourced** real imagery: vase paintings, site photography (Troy), Linear B tablets, manuscripts, historical portraits/busts. Not a live indexed library — each must be fetched via the picture library / extractors.
58	- `text-overlay` — typography-driven scenes: epithet refrains, "Notice…" key-insight beats, section markers, the Achilles quote
59	- `graphic` — maps (seven cities, the voyage), the historical timeline, small explanatory diagrams
60	
61	Target distribution for this project (weighted away from the template default because the library is empty): ~45–55% `generated-image`, ~15–20% `b-roll` (sourced), ~15–20% `text-overlay`, ~15% `graphic`. If museum sourcing underdelivers, absorb the shortfall into `generated-image`, not stock.
62	
63	## Provenance
64	
65	- **Descended from template:** `essay-doc-explainer-v1`, version 1 (match score 0.890 — exact genre, in-range duration, strong text overlap).
66	
67	This project keeps the template's explainer-documentary spine: conversational-but-rigorous voice, educational-not-advocacy posture on a live debate, the four-mode look, section-marker discipline, and enforced visual-type variety. Three things changed for Homer specifically. First, the multi-part numbered thesis was dropped — this script runs a single controlling metaphor ("Homer is the Ithaca we never reach"), so the guide directs visual reprises of that motif instead of a "three lenses" structure. Second, the cadence phrase "here's the key insight" was replaced with the script's own recurring "Notice…/Now watch…" invitation to observe the blind poet's choices, and the "quote named modern people" move was re-pointed at the poem's own epithets and Achilles' underworld line, since the script cites Schliemann/Calvert/Ventris/Wolf for their actions but never quotes them. Third, and most consequentially, the source library is empty: the `Look` and vocabulary were rebalanced to make `generated-image` the dominant mode, redefine `b-roll` as deliberately sourced museum/site imagery, and forbid promising archival footage that doesn't exist. Pacing windows were re-proportioned to the actual 8:05 runtime rather than the template's 10-minute default.
68

- **Echo the single controlling metaphor visually**, not a fake multi-part structure. Reprise the receding-figure-over-water motif at the thesis, the Book 8 mirror, and the close so the through-line reads.
- **No sustained talking-heads / no lecture stills** — always intercut generated imagery, vase art, or graphics.
- **Hold the even hand on the "forgery" debate.** Visuals should dramatize scholarly complexity (rival manuscripts, layered oral tradition), never caricature the online skeptics. Symbolic yes; mockery no.

## Visual Type Vocabulary

Tuned to this project. `generated-image` is promoted to the primary mode because there is no indexed footage; `b-roll` is retained but redefined for sourced museum/site imagery rather than a live library.

- `generated-image` — AI-generated mythic/cinematic stills and short-animated imagery (the dominant mode)
- `b-roll` — **sourced** real imagery: vase paintings, site photography (Troy), Linear B tablets, manuscripts, historical portraits/busts. Not a live indexed library — each must be fetched via the picture library / extractors.
- `text-overlay` — typography-driven scenes: epithet refrains, "Notice…" key-insight beats, section markers, the Achilles quote
- `graphic` — maps (seven cities, the voyage), the historical timeline, small explanatory diagrams

Target distribution for this project (weighted away from the template default because the library is empty): ~45–55% `generated-image`, ~15–20% `b-roll` (sourced), ~15–20% `text-overlay`, ~15% `graphic`. If museum sourcing underdelivers, absorb the shortfall into `generated-image`, not stock.

## Provenance

- **Descended from template:** `essay-doc-explainer-v1`, version 1 (match score 0.890 — exact genre, in-range duration, strong text overlap).

This project keeps the template's explainer-documentary spine: conversational-but-rigorous voice, educational-not-advocacy posture on a live debate, the four-mode look, section-marker discipline, and enforced visual-type variety. Three things changed for Homer specifically. First, the multi-part numbered thesis was dropped — this script runs a single controlling metaphor ("Homer is the Ithaca we never reach"), so the guide directs visual reprises of that motif instead of a "three lenses" structure. Second, the cadence phrase "here's the key insight" was replaced with the script's own recurring "Notice…/Now watch…" invitation to observe the blind poet's choices, and the "quote named modern people" move was re-pointed at the poem's own epithets and Achilles' underworld line, since the script cites Schliemann/Calvert/Ventris/Wolf for their actions but never quotes them. Third, and most consequentially, the source library is empty: the `Look` and vocabulary were rebalanced to make `generated-image` the dominant mode, redefine `b-roll` as deliberately sourced museum/site imagery, and forbid promising archival footage that doesn't exist. Pacing windows were re-proportioned to the actual 8:05 runtime rather than the template's 10-minute default.

{
  "sections": {
    "Hook — the poet nobody can find": [
      {
        "id": "scene_001",
        "start": "0:00",
        "duration": "6s",
        "narration_excerpt": "Seven cities of the ancient world each swore that Homer was born on their soil — and not one of them could produce the man.",
        "visual_type": "graphic",
        "visual_description": "Section-opening map card: an antique-styled map of the Aegean with seven cities (Smyrna, Chios, Colophon, Salamis, Rhodes, Argos, Athens) each glowing and labelled, each with a claiming banner — but the space where a portrait should sit stays empty. Serif title treatment."
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_002",
        "start": "0:06",
        "duration": "6s",
        "narration_excerpt": "He is said to have been blind, though the sheer visual dazzle of his verse makes even that doubtful.",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "Close portrait of an aged blind bard, eyes clouded and unseeing, yet the darkness around his face carries faint reflections of vivid scenes — ships, fire, sea — as if he sees everything inwardly.",
        "comfyui_prompt": "cinematic close-up portrait of an ancient blind Greek bard, milky clouded eyes, weathered face, faint dreamlike reflections of ships and wine-dark sea shimmering in the surrounding darkness, chiaroscuro torchlight, mythic photorealism, 35mm, shallow depth of field"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_003",
        "start": "0:12",
        "duration": "6s",
        "narration_excerpt": "And lately a chorus of voices online has gone further still, insisting that Homer never lived at all,",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "Symbolic composition: pages of ancient verse dissolving and scattering into fragments, no human figure present — the author erased, only floating text-shards drifting apart.",
        "comfyui_prompt": "aged parchment pages of ancient Greek verse breaking apart into drifting fragments, ink dissolving into dark void, no figure, symbolic and cinematic, cold blue-grey palette, moody atmosphere, high detail"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_004",
        "start": "0:18",
        "duration": "6s",
        "narration_excerpt": "that the epics are a forgery stitched together by later hands.",
        "visual_type": "graphic",
        "visual_description": "Diagrammatic card: torn manuscript strips being stitched together by dotted seams, with the word “FORGERY?” set in serif type, question mark emphasized to keep the even hand rather than assert the claim."
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_005",
        "start": "0:24",
        "duration": "6s",
        "narration_excerpt": "It is a strange accusation to level at the two poems Western literature was built on.",
        "visual_type": "b-roll",
        "visual_description": "Museum-grade still of an early illuminated or papyrus manuscript page of the Iliad/Odyssey, Greek text visible, treated as an artifact under gallery light.",
        "search_query": "ancient Greek Odyssey Iliad manuscript papyrus page museum"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_006",
        "start": "0:30",
        "duration": "6s",
        "narration_excerpt": "Stranger still when you realize the poem answers it — because tucked into the eighth book of the Odyssey, singing at a feast, is a blind bard named Demodocus,",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "A torchlit Bronze-Age Phaeacian hall at a feast; a blind bard sits with a lyre, mid-song, the crowd leaning in around him.",
        "comfyui_prompt": "torchlit Bronze-Age Greek banquet hall, a blind bard playing a lyre and singing at a feast, seated nobles listening, warm firelight, cinematic mythic realism, volumetric light, painterly detail"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_007",
        "start": "0:36",
        "duration": "7s",
        "narration_excerpt": "whose song moves the hall to tears while the man himself sits unseen in the corner.",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "Same hall, focus shifts to a nameless cloaked stranger (Odysseus) weeping quietly in shadow while the bard remains unnoticed in the corner — the song more present than either man.",
        "comfyui_prompt": "a cloaked stranger weeping silently in the shadowed corner of a torchlit ancient Greek feast hall, tears on his face, a blind bard barely visible singing beyond him, emotional cinematic lighting, mythic realism, shallow focus"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_008",
        "start": "0:43",
        "duration": "6s",
        "narration_excerpt": "Homer, it seems, painted his own portrait",
        "visual_type": "b-roll",
        "visual_description": "The idealized ancient marble bust of the blind “Homer,” shot as a museum still, sightless carved eyes catching gallery light.",
        "search_query": "ancient marble bust of Homer blind poet museum sculpture"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_009",
        "start": "0:49",
        "duration": "6s",
        "narration_excerpt": "and hid it inside his masterpiece.",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "Recurring motif established: a solitary figure receding across wine-dark water toward the horizon, small and unreachable — the through-line image of the whole essay.",
        "comfyui_prompt": "a lone silhouetted figure in a small boat receding across a vast wine-dark sea toward the horizon at dusk, tiny against endless water, atmospheric haze, cinematic wide shot, mythic melancholy, deep reds and indigo"
      }
    ],
    "Thesis": [
      {
        "id": "scene_010",
        "start": "0:55",
        "duration": "10s",
        "narration_excerpt": "The Odyssey is the first and greatest story ever told about coming home.",
        "visual_type": "text-overlay",
        "visual_description": "Section-opening thesis card: the line set large in a literary serif face, centered on a dark wine-dark-sea gradient — the controlling statement given room to register."
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_011",
        "start": "1:05",
        "duration": "10s",
        "narration_excerpt": "And its author is the one figure in it who never arrives — a voice over the water, forever just out of reach.",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "Reprise of the receding-figure motif: the distant voice/figure over water, this time framed so the viewer is straining after it — the author who never docks.",
        "comfyui_prompt": "distant lone figure barely visible on the wine-dark sea, seen from shore, forever out of reach at the horizon, soft evening light, atmospheric perspective, cinematic mythic realism, longing mood"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_012",
        "start": "1:15",
        "duration": "10s",
        "narration_excerpt": "What Ithaca is to Odysseus, Homer is to us. We spend the whole poem straining for a shore we will never quite touch.",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "Viewer-POV shot from a boat straining toward a distant island shore (Ithaca) that never seems to grow nearer — the unreachable homecoming as metaphor.",
        "comfyui_prompt": "first person point of view from the prow of an ancient boat straining toward a distant Greek island shore that remains far away, calm sea, golden hour haze, cinematic longing, photoreal mythic atmosphere"
      }
    ],
    "The life that isn't — and the mirror in Book 8": [
      {
        "id": "scene_013",
        "start": "1:25",
        "duration": "9s",
        "narration_excerpt": "Every great book seems to come with a life to explain it — a childhood wound, a lost love, a war.",
        "visual_type": "text-overlay",
        "visual_description": "Section-marker card “The life that isn't” in serif type, with the narration line beneath as a subtitle refrain over a dim textured background."
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_014",
        "start": "1:34",
        "duration": "9s",
        "narration_excerpt": "Homer gives us almost nothing.",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "An empty stone pedestal in a museum niche where a portrait bust should stand — a blank, dust-lit absence standing in for the missing biography.",
        "comfyui_prompt": "an empty stone pedestal in a shadowed museum niche where a portrait bust should be, dust motes in a single shaft of light, absence and emptiness, cinematic, muted stone palette, photoreal"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_015",
        "start": "1:43",
        "duration": "10s",
        "narration_excerpt": "Around 700 BC, twenty-eight centuries ago, someone shaped these lines into their final grandeur —",
        "visual_type": "graphic",
        "visual_description": "Timeline card placing a single marker at ~700 BC on a long horizontal timeline (with “28 centuries ago” annotation), the rest of the line fading into uncertainty at both ends."
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_016",
        "start": "1:53",
        "duration": "9s",
        "narration_excerpt": "but that someone dissolves the moment you reach for him. He may not even have been a single person.",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "A shadowy human figure dissolving into many overlapping silhouettes as a hand reaches toward it — the author fragmenting into a tradition of many.",
        "comfyui_prompt": "a shadowy human figure dissolving and splitting into many overlapping translucent silhouettes as a hand reaches toward it, dark atmospheric void, symbolic, cinematic, cold desaturated palette, high detail"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_017",
        "start": "2:02",
        "duration": "10s",
        "narration_excerpt": "And so, denied a biography, we look instead at what Homer chose to put on the page.",
        "visual_type": "b-roll",
        "visual_description": "Museum still of a page of the Odyssey's Greek text (Book 8), close on the columns of script, treated as evidence we turn to in place of a life.",
        "search_query": "Homer Odyssey ancient Greek manuscript text page book 8"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_018",
        "start": "2:12",
        "duration": "10s",
        "narration_excerpt": "He gave us Demodocus — a poet the Muse had touched, as the poem says, by taking his eyes and granting him \"the sweet singing art.\"",
        "visual_type": "text-overlay",
        "visual_description": "Epithet-refrain card: “the sweet singing art” set in the recurring serif refrain style, with “Demodocus” named beneath — establishing the poem's own language as texture."
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_019",
        "start": "2:22",
        "duration": "11s",
        "narration_excerpt": "At the Phaeacian court, Demodocus sings of the fall of Troy, of the wooden horse and the cleverest of the Greeks —",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "The Phaeacian court listening as the bard sings; his song visualized above the hall as a spectral image of the great wooden horse before the walls of burning Troy.",
        "comfyui_prompt": "a blind bard singing in a Phaeacian royal hall, above him a spectral vision of the giant wooden horse before the burning walls of Troy, layered dreamlike composition, torchlight below and firelight vision above, cinematic mythic realism"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_020",
        "start": "2:33",
        "duration": "10s",
        "narration_excerpt": "not knowing that the cleverest of the Greeks is sitting right in front of him, a nameless stranger. And Odysseus pulls his cloak over his head and weeps.",
        "visual_type": "text-overlay",
        "visual_description": "Quiet insight card over a dim image of a hooded weeping figure: type notes that the man the song is about sits unrecognized in the room — the poem's mirror moment stated plainly."
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_021",
        "start": "2:43",
        "duration": "8s",
        "narration_excerpt": "It is the whole poem in miniature. The singer is blind. The song is more alive in the room than the man it's about.",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "A luminous, almost living current of song filling the torchlit hall while both the blind singer and the cloaked man recede into shadow — the song outshining its makers.",
        "comfyui_prompt": "a glowing luminous current of music and voice filling a torchlit ancient hall, the blind singer and a cloaked listener fading into shadow at the edges, the song rendered as radiant living light, symbolic cinematic mythic realism"
      },
      {
        "id": "scene_022",
        "start": "2:51",
        "duration": "9s",
        "narration_excerpt": "We could say that here, in a nameless bard, Homer tells us exactly who he is — a voice that outlasts its body, a maker who vanishes into what he makes.",
        "visual_type": "generated-image",
        "visual_description": "Reprise of the through-line motif at the Book 8 mirror: the bard's silhouette thinning into the receding-voice-over-water image — the maker dissolving into his own song.",
